primary_diagnosis.name: Acute urinary retention secondary to benign prostatic hyperplasia, precipitated by an alpha-adrenergic decongestant (pseudoephedrine)

pathophysiology_summary: Longstanding benign prostatic hyperplasia produces a fixed bladder-outlet obstruction (the 6-month history of hesitancy, poor stream and nocturia). Recent ingestion of pseudoephedrine, an alpha-1 adrenergic agonist, increases smooth-muscle tone at the bladder neck and prostatic urethra, tipping a compensated obstruction into complete failure to void — acute urinary retention. The bladder fills to a large volume (palpation suggests >1000 mL) producing a tense, painful, palpable suprapubic mass and intense detrusor/visceral pain with a relentless urge. Overflow (paradoxical) incontinence occurs as intravesical pressure exceeds outlet resistance. Sustained high intravesical pressure is transmitted retrogradely to the upper tracts, risking hydronephrosis and post-renal (obstructive) acute kidney injury, and, rarely, bladder-wall ischaemia/rupture. Immediate decompression by urethral catheterisation is the definitive treatment; the tachycardia and hypertension are a sympathetic pain response that resolves on decompression.

differential.evidence_basis: Absolute inability to void for 14 hours with severe suprapubic pain and a relentless urge is the classic presentation of acute retention.
Also causes lower-urinary distress, but cystitis produces frequent, small-volume, painful VOIDING — not absolute inability to pass urine with a distended bladder.
Severe pain, but colic is typically unilateral flank pain radiating to the groin with preserved voiding, not central suprapubic pain with a palpable full bladder.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, retention should already lead given absolute anuria plus suprapubic pain; UTI and colic are reasonable to hold until the discriminators (inability to void + palpable bladder) are confirmed.

differential.diagnosis: acute urinary retention secondary to BPH, decongestant-precipitated
differential.evidence_basis: 6-month obstructive LUTS + new pseudoephedrine + absolute retention with distended bladder pins the mechanism.
Infection can coexist and can itself precipitate retention; must be screened (fever, dysuria) and covered if the urine is infected once drained.
A loaded rectum can compress the bladder outlet and mimic/aggravate retention; less likely with a clearly bladder-centred palpable mass.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The medication history (pseudoephedrine) is the highest-yield discriminator of mechanism and is a scored critical item.

differential.diagnosis: acute urinary retention secondary to BPH, decongestant-precipitated
differential.evidence_basis: Diagnostic tetrad: absolute inability to void, severe suprapubic pain, palpable distended bladder to the umbilicus, and marked distress — on a background of BPH with a decongestant trigger. Confirmed in-person by a benign enlarged prostate on DRE, bladder volume >1000 mL, and instant relief on catheterisation.
No fever/rigors and the picture is obstruction with a full bladder, not frequent painful small voids — though drained urine should be tested.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Certainty achievable via telehealth is presumptive_pending_workup — the AI cannot palpate, do a DRE, scan the bladder or catheterise; it should reach a strong presumptive diagnosis and escalate for decompression.

red_flags.description: Absolute inability to pass urine for 14 hours with a tense, distended, painful bladder — acute urinary retention requiring urgent decompression
red_flags.clinical_significance: Unrelieved retention transmits high pressure to the kidneys, causing post-renal acute kidney injury and hydronephrosis, with severe ongoing pain and rare risk of bladder-wall injury.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive obstructive acute kidney injury, worsening pain, and potential upper-tract damage if decompression is delayed.

red_flags.description: Recent alpha-adrenergic decongestant (pseudoephedrine) as the precipitant on a background of BPH
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifies a reversible, avoidable trigger; continued or repeated decongestant use risks recurrent retention.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed medication review means the patient may take the same or similar OTC decongestants again and re-present with retention.

red_flags.description: Signs that would indicate infected obstruction / obstructive pyelonephritis or urosepsis — fever, rigors, flank or back pain (currently absent but must be safety-netted)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An obstructed, infected urinary tract is a surgical emergency; fever or rigors with retention escalates urgency sharply.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected infected obstruction can progress to septic shock within hours.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'urinary problem = infection' and treating for cystitis; the discriminator is ABSOLUTE inability to void with a palpable full bladder, not frequent small painful voids.
Taking the small overflow dribble as evidence the patient is still passing urine, and under-triaging — overflow incontinence signals a critically full, high-pressure bladder, not relief.
Failing to take an OTC medication history and so missing the pseudoephedrine precipitant, losing both the mechanism and the key preventive advice.
Advising the patient to wait for a routine clinic; acute retention needs same-day catheterisation to relieve pain and protect the kidneys.

Attempting to talk the patient through self-relief measures (hot shower, running taps) as definitive management instead of escalating for catheterisation — these may be tried briefly but must not delay escalation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation and percussion of the suprapubic mass and its extent
Digital rectal examination of the prostate (size, consistency, nodules)
Bladder volume by bedside ultrasound (>400 mL; palpation suggests >1000 mL)
Blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and other measured vital signs
Examination of the urethral meatus for stricture or discharge
Serum creatinine / renal function and urinalysis of drained urine
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise acute urinary retention and arrange URGENT same-day bladder decompression by urethral catheterisation — direct the patient to an emergency department (or an after-hours/urgent service able to catheterise) now; call 000 if he cannot get there because of pain or being alone, or if any fever/rigors/flank pain develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the syndrome (the history is characteristic); the real risk is UNDER-action — under-triaging to a routine appointment, mislabelling as UTI, or attempting home self-relief instead of arranging catheterisation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: General practice / urology guidance on acute urinary retention (e.g. Therapeutic Guidelines; EAU/urological society AUR guidance)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute urinary retention is relieved by immediate urethral (or suprapubic) catheterisation; identify and remove precipitants (including alpha-agonist decongestants and anticholinergics); commence an alpha-blocker and plan a trial without catheter.
